note: Annual spending areas used to give scale to debt and debt interest. Comparisons help readers picture size only; they do not imply budgets could be redirected to or from debt. Scope varies by area (see each note). Figures are estimates pending line-by-line verification against HM Treasury statistics.

areas.name: NHS / health
areas.source_name: House of Commons Library, Main Estimates 2025-26 (DHSC)
areas.notes: Total Department of Health and Social Care budget for England, 2025-26 (Resource DEL about £208bn plus Capital DEL about £14bn). The NHS England core budget alone is about £152bn.

areas.name: Education
areas.source_name: HM Treasury, Public Expenditure Statistical Analyses 2026 (Table 5.2)
areas.notes: Total UK public education spending, 2025-26 outturn (£125.7bn), published in PESA 2026 on 8 July 2026. Part of the rise on the previous £120.8bn figure is revision between PESA editions rather than higher spending. The DfE day-to-day budget for England alone is about £101bn.

areas.name: Defence
areas.source_name: HM Treasury, Public Expenditure Statistical Analyses 2026 (Table 1.9)
areas.notes: MoD total departmental budget (Total DEL) for 2025-26, £61,994m outturn (about 2% of GDP). On the broader NATO definition, which adds items such as pensions and security funds, UK spending is higher; the government plans to reach 2.5% of GDP by 2027 and 3.5% by 2035. The COFOG defence function is different again, at £65.4bn.

areas.name: State pension
areas.source_name: DWP benefit expenditure tables / OBR November 2025
areas.notes: State pension expenditure forecast for 2025-26 (about £146.1bn), paid to over 12 million pensioners. Up sharply on earlier years, driven by the triple lock.

areas.name: Welfare and social protection (total)
areas.source_name: DWP benefit expenditure tables / HM Treasury
areas.notes: Total social security spending (DWP benefits, tax credits, Child Benefit). Includes the state pension shown separately above. The broader social-protection function is about £407bn (PESA 2026, 2025-26 outturn).

areas.name: Central government receipts
areas.source_name: ONS, Public sector finances, UK: April 2026
areas.notes: Central government total current receipts, financial year ending March 2026 (£1,122.8bn). This is everything central government took in, so it is more than total tax receipts, which are £1,108bn on a National Accounts basis (OBR March 2026), and less than total public sector current receipts, which are around £1,235bn.
